content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompts to Mitigate Social Biases in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit social biases and stereotypes in their outputs, reflecting societal prejudices present in their training data. Existing prompting methods insufficiently mitigate these biases in open-ended or few-shot tasks.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, reducing their propagation of harmful stereotypes is critical for fairness and trust. Prompt-based mitigation is attractive because it requires no model retraining and can be broadly applied. Current prompt engineering approaches lack mechanisms for explicit self-reflection or counterfactual reasoning, which humans often use to check biases.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method that appends a `Counterfactual Self-Reflection` instruction to user queries. After generating an initial response, the LLM is prompted to self-reflect and generate a counterfactual version: 'If I were to avoid any social biases or stereotypes, how would my previous answer change?' The model then outputs a revised answer, guided by its internal critique. This dual-step prompt can be formatted generically for any task (e.g., story generation, QA) and operates as post-hoc bias reduction without labeled bias data.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate this method on established bias benchmarks such as the StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs datasets, as well as open-domain QA with demographic references. Baselines will include direct prompting, instruction-tuned bias-mitigation prompts, and post-processing approaches. Metrics will focus on bias scores (e.g., stereotype, anti-stereotype, unrelated), answer quality (fluency, informativeness), and human evaluations of bias reduction."
}
